sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
A seizure is abnormal electrical activity in the brain causing transient changes in behaviour, movement, sensation, or awareness. These discharges are typically spontaneous, uncontrolled and transitory. Seizures are a sign of an underlying disorder.
- Idiopathic (self-originating and without known cause, most epileptics)
- Symptomatic (known medical or traumatic cause) Status epilepticus (SE) is defined as:
  - A seizure lasting >5 minutes, or
  - Recurrent seizures without return to baseline consciousness between episodes.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Motor: Tonic-clonic movements (stiffening/jerking), focal limb twitching, head nodding
- Non-motor: Staring, confusion, lip-smacking, sensory hallucinations, unresponsiveness
- Autonomic: Tachycardia, hypersalivation, apnoea, incontinence
- Post-ictal phase: Fatigue, confusion, headache, amnesia
- Prolonged seizures or SE can be associated with hypoxia, hypercarbia, lactic/respiratory acidosis, hyperthermia, hypertension, tachycardia, hypo/hyperglycaemia, hyperkalaemia

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Anticipate potential airway obstruction; while tongue trauma typically poses minimal risk, dental fracture carries significant aspiration hazard
- Midazolam can be administered intravenously, intramuscularly, intranasally, or via the buccal routes
- Pharmacological management of non-convulsive or atypical seizure types requires consultation with the on-call doctor or RFDS medical support
- Implement environmental safety measures by removing hazards and providing head protection with padding. Apply physical restraint only when necessary to prevent immediate injury
- Continuous airway assessment and intervention are required throughout ictal and post- ictal phases to ensure patency and prevent aspiration
- Generalised seizure activity should be considered an emergency where:
  - The seizure is prolonged (> 5 minutes), or
  - Multiple seizures occur, or
  - The patient doesn’t regain consciousness

**Focal seizure activity associated where:**
  - The seizure is prolonged (> 5 minutes) and is associated with a GCS ≤ 12
  - Common causes: Epilepsy, hypoglycaemia, CNS infection, head trauma, drug toxicity, stroke, alcohol withdrawal
  - Red flags: First seizure, trauma during seizure, signs of infection (fever, rash), persistent post-ictal unresponsiveness

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Protect from injury whilst the patient is seizing
- If the seizure is prolonged or recurrent, consider midazolam via the approved route and dose under local protocol and in consultation with on-call doctor where required
- Complete Primary Survey
- Open, clear, and maintain the airway where possible. Never put fingers or objects in the patient's mouth during active seizure activity
- Suction if required and where possible
- Insert an appropriate airway adjunct once safe to do so; consider NPA if trismus is present
- Lateral position if unresponsive (use left lateral position for pregnant women)
- Oxygen with assisted ventilation if indicated
- Identify and address the cause where possible
- Full Vital Signs Survey, particularly GCS, BGL, Pulse Oximetry and Temperature
- Note seizure onset time and total duration
- Complete Secondary Survey once seizure activity has terminated
- Limb lead ECG, being mindful of muscular artefact
- Consider urinalysis where appropriate, e.g. in pregnancy
- ESOs, MESOs and other similar levels should have a low threshold for calling the on-call doctor

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Establish vascular access
- Consider blood draw, where appropriate, for i-STAT testing: Chem8 (lactate and electrolytes)
- Consider IV midazolam, titrated to effect, in consultation with on-call doctor
- Consider IV glucose if related to low BGL

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Complications and side effects of seizures can include:
  - Incontinence
  - Secondary injury (including intra-oral trauma)
  - Aspiration
  - Hypoxia
  - Hyperthermia
  - Dysrhythmias
  - Brain damage
  - Death
  - ESO’s, MESOs and other similar levels should have a low threshold for calling the current poison permit holder or RFDS
